title: TPU v1 · External I/O and Connectivity with PCIe + DDR3
oneLine: TPU reduces external I/O to two trunks: PCIe to the host, because it is a host-driven coprocessor, and a DDR3 PHY to off-chip weight memory. Larger-scale expansion is left to ICI and optical switching in later TPU pods.

legend.name: PCIe 3.0 (to host)
legend.desc: TPU v1 is a server-attached coprocessor. PCIe 3.0 ×16, at about 16 GB/s in each direction, carries host-issued CISC commands and data. The small instruction set—about a dozen commands—keeps interface traffic modest.

legend.name: PCIe interface / command queue
legend.desc: The card's PCIe endpoint and control interface queue host commands for the compute core. In a host-driven model, the chip does not fetch instructions autonomously; it waits for the host.

legend.name: DDR3 memory-interface PHY
legend.desc: The PHY connects on-chip logic to 8 GiB of off-chip DDR3 and brings weights into the chip. Its ~30 GB/s bandwidth (paper Table 1) is a frequent v1 inference bottleneck.

legend.name: Off-chip 8 GiB DDR3
legend.desc: Large-capacity storage for model weights. Capacity is sufficient, but bandwidth is low relative to compute—v1's central I/O tradeoff.

legend.name: Scale-out (not in v1)
legend.desc: TPU v1 is a single card without a high-speed chip-to-chip link. Later TPU v2/v3 pods scale through ICI links and OCS optical switching; that is family evolution, not a v1 feature.

steps.cap: ① The host pushes CISC commands and input activations over PCIe 3.0; TPU is a passive coprocessor driven by the host.
② The card's PCIe interface queues commands for the MXU and Unified Buffer.
③ The compute core fetches weights from 8 GiB of DDR3 through the PHY at ~30 GB/s, a common I/O bottleneck.
④ Results return to the host over PCIe, completing one inference request.
⑤ Beyond one card, v1 has no chip-to-chip fabric; later pods add ICI and optical circuit switching (OCS).

before: Define external connections: What must the chip connect to, through which interfaces, and at what bandwidth? TPU v1 is intentionally restrained: only a host over PCIe and off-chip weight memory over DDR3.
Decisions to make: PCIe generation and lane count, memory-interface type and width, target I/O bandwidths, system role (standalone SoC or coprocessor), and whether chip-to-chip links are required.
Required inputs: Weight capacity and delivery cadence from the workload profile (§3.1), the deployment form factor—an add-in card for existing servers—and how TensorFlow/XLA submits work.
Hard constraints: Compatibility with deployed servers favors mature PCIe; large weight capacity requires off-chip DRAM; and package pins, power, and area limit memory width and PHY count.
during: Choose the system role: Make TPU a coprocessor rather than a standalone system. Reusing host CPU, networking, and storage reduces external connectivity to PCIe and simplifies deployment in existing servers.

Select the host interface: Mature PCIe 3.0 fits a host-driven model in which the host issues a few coarse CISC commands and the chip avoids complex fetch and scheduling logic. The tradeoff is dependence on host timing.
Select the memory interface: Model weights exceed on-chip capacity, requiring external DRAM. Choose 8 GiB of DDR3 and prioritize capacity, with a DDR3 PHY at the physical boundary.

Confront the bandwidth tradeoff: ~30 GB/s of DDR3 is low beside ~92 TOPS of compute—the v1 memory wall. A 24 MiB Unified Buffer and weight-stationary reuse mitigate it, but weight-heavy, small-batch workloads remain I/O-bound.
Defer scale-out: v1 invests in a single card, not high-speed chip-to-chip connectivity. Directly connected pods appear in v2/v3 through ICI and OCS, outside v1's scope.

State disclosure limits: PCIe endpoint, DDR PHY protocol, coherency, and link-training details follow standard data-center ASIC practice where the v1 paper is silent. Only published bandwidth figures are treated as v1 facts.

result.label: Scaling model
result.value: Single-card v1; v2/v3 pods use ICI + OCS

result.label: I/O philosophy
result.value: Minimize interfaces; keep complexity inside the chip

after: Validate I/O performance: Measure PCIe and DDR3 utilization on real workloads to identify compute versus I/O limits. Many measured v1 workloads are memory- or PCIe-bound rather than peak-compute-bound.
Propagate physical needs: Specify PHY count and width for package pins, I/O rails for power integrity, and PHY placement/routing constraints for physical implementation (⑤).
Hand off to system integration: Confirm the card fits target servers, trains and enumerates on PCIe, and exchanges tasks correctly with TensorFlow/XLA.
Add test and observability hooks: Include PCIe/DDR3 bandwidth and stall counters plus standard DFT and loopback for I/O PHYs; these details are general ASIC practice, not disclosed v1 mechanisms.
Plan the next generation: Scale-out requires chip-to-chip ICI and OCS switching—the direction taken by TPU v2/v3 pods.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (PCIe and DDR3 bandwidths are published; ~30 GB/s for weight memory follows Table 1. PHY/link-training details are standard data-center ASIC practice, not itemized for v1; ICI/OCS pods apply to TPU v2/v3.)
